text: Gus drags the fuel hose toward the Q400's wing and snaps the nozzle into the underwing port.
Birdy walks over with a coil of bonding cable already in his hand and a clip on the end.
Hold your start a sec, I'll get the bond on before you flow.
It's on. Front bumper, same as always.
Birdy crouches at the truck's frame and follows the cable down to the clip.
Want it on bare metal, not paint. Takes ten seconds.
I'm two turns behind, Birdy. It's fine.
Gus thumbs the deadman and the pump kicks over with a low whine.
Birdy reaches under the truck and lifts the clamp; it slides free off a painted bracket with no bite at all.
Gus. This is sitting on paint and it shook loose. You're flowing with no bond.
It's always fine. Forty years that hose never lit up.
Fuel's moving right now. Stop it for me.
I am not stopping my turn for a ramper. Clip it back and quit hovering.
Birdy steps to the deadman in Gus's fist and presses it; the pump winds down and the hose goes slack.
Gus stares at the dead nozzle.
Are you kidding me.
Birdy works the clamp open, scrapes it onto the bare steel of the frame rail, and clicks it tight.
There. Good metal now. You can go again.
A baggage cart driver near the cargo door has stopped to watch.
Gus keys his radio without looking at Birdy.
Yeah, ramp, fueler at gate eleven. Your handler shut my pump down in the middle of a turn. I'm gonna blow the slot.
Tanner crosses the ramp with a tablet, eyes on the screen.
Who stopped the fueling.
That was me.
You hit the deadman on an active fuel.
Bond was off the metal. I reset it.
We're nine minutes over. You don't stop a turn, you flag a fueler. That's a write-up.
Tanner taps the tablet and turns it for Birdy to sign.
Hose was fine.
Birdy signs with one finger and hands the tablet back.
Well. Adds a little excitement to the gas station.
Don't do it again.
Yes sir.
Tanner walks off reading the clock.
Gus thumbs the deadman and the pump spins back up, fuel running through the clamped bond.
Birdy picks up his bag tags and heads back to the cart.
